Columbia Defeats the P.C. Separatists
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Some of my fellow students at Columbia staged a two-week protest this month to try to force the university to establish an ethnic studies department. Such a department would be wrong. Twenty-two students were arrested after more than 150 occupied Low Library, the main administrative building. About 100 blocked the central classroom building for four days to protest the school's "hegemonic and  white supremacist" curriculum. 

     While I admire such activism and dedication, these tactics only disrupted the education for which we pay so much. Many students disagreed, not only with the means used but with the ends demanded.

Here's a Stadium Plan That Will Work
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Faced with shrinking revenues and cuts in services, New York City has to find ways to grow. Done a little differently, Mayor Rudolph Giuliani's proposal to build a ball park over the Long Island Rail Road yards in the West 30's offers this chance -- without dislocating residents or businesses. 

     In fact, it's a mistake to cast such a proposal as a sports project. The goal should not be simply to create a stadium but to develop an engine of growth that can create jobs and revenues for years to come.

Could We Be Importing the Ebola Virus?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Once again, the Ebola virus has appeared in monkeys in the United States. This time, monkeys imported from the Philippines for medical research were found to be infected with Ebola-Reston, a strain that is not dangerous to humans, at a primate center in Alice, Tex. 

     In 1989, the same virus turned up in monkeys at a research laboratory in Reston, Va. After that scare, officials at the Centers for Disease Control promised that improved quarantine measures would protect the country from a recurrence.

VOICES: VIEWPOINT;We Need a Ron Brown Award for Business
nytimes_articles.abstract:
COMMERCE Secretary Ronald H. Brown was an extraordinarily effective ambassador for our country's business community, and he will be missed. 

     But we can commemorate both his life and his belief that business can be a force for good by establishing an award that would be granted -- as a parallel to the Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Awards -- to our nation's top exporters and most thoughtful, responsible overseas investors.

Asia-Pacific Issue;Journey to a Sacred Mount
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ALTHOUGH Mount Kailas, at 22,028 feet, is not one of the highest Himalayan peaks, it is among the world's most beautiful mountains. A delicately curved pyramid with a rounded top, its most striking feature is concentric bands of dark rock on which snow accumulates, forming stripes of black and white. 

     But it is something beyond physical beauty that, for more than a thousand years, has drawn pilgrims, who perform a kora -- a 32-mile walk around the mountain, ascending almost 5,000 feet along the way -- to pay homage to the gods. Kailas, the most sacred mountain in mainland Asia, is considered the home  of gods of three faiths: Hinduism, Buddhism and Bon, the ancient religion predating Buddhism in Tibet.

SOAPBOX;'Take Our Daughters'? Always Have
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN the Virgin Islands, my mother worked as a maid for Mr. Sebastian, the European proprietor of a white-owned store on Main Street. As a small boy, I'd come to work with her each day; I remember that she once slapped my face after I'd used his bathroom. (I later understood that I shouldn't have flushed: water  was scarce on the island. But how was I to know? We blacks didn't have indoor plumbing at all.) 

     I think about Mr. Sebastian's house whenever I think about Take Our Daughters to Work Day, which is to be observed on Thursday. This is the fourth such observance; the day was started in 1993 by the Ms. Foundation for Women as a way for girls aged 9 to 15 to see what goes on in the working world.

L.I. VINES
And Miles to Go
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE older I get, the more I love to travel. I've always wanted to see the world, but never so urgently as since I turned 50 and faced up to the mathematical discrepancy between the years I have left to live and the places I  have left to see. 

     Middle-aged travel lust may well be as developmentally predictable as hearing loss, bifocals and midriff bulge. For those who can afford it, traveling for pleasure can become a detour from the downhill jitters, an antidote to existential angst, and the best revenge against aging since collagen.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;Help Is Just A Keystroke Away! Sure.
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE latest computer programs used in businesses and homes are, indeed, miracles of sophistication and power. 

     But knowing little of how such miracles are wrought, it puzzles me to observe that, as the software has become more complex, the user guides have become slimmer. As a crude comparison, the manual for my old Microsoft Windows, version 3.1, is seven-eighths of an inch thick, while my Windows 95 manual measures seven-sixteenths of an inch, albeit with an ancillary publication, introducing me to the operating system, at three-sixteenths of an inch. Compare this with the hearty 2-inch-thick book, from an independent source, that I had to purchase to understand Windows 95, and you have to wonder what is happening.

QUICK BITE;How About Some Fried Rice With Your Tandoori Chicken?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Manchurian vegetable soup, sweet and sour chicken and fried rice: not exactly what you expect to find at an Indian restaurant. But dishes like these show up -- in an-only-on-Sunday twist -- on the lunch buffet at Mohgul in Morristown. 

     A few diners are surprised to find Chinese appetizers and entrees alongside traditional Indian favorites like lamb masala and tandoori chicken. But Moghul's owners, Sneh and Satish Mehtani, explain that the milder Chinese cuisine was a treat in their native India, and many regular customers requested it.

Below Stairs
Fun at the Gym: On the Balance Beam, Doing Cartwheels
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FAIRFIELD COUNTY GYMNASTICS, in Fairfield, has been in existence for 14 years and has a membership of 375. The owner and founder, Elaine Audy, has seen her youngsters achieve goals, gain self-confidence and have fun, which is the real point. "Gymnastics should enhance their lives, not kick everything else out," she said. "You don't have to be a star." To her, the Olympics has become too focused on the individual and the gymnasts are portrayed badly when they fail. "I highlight the kids' successes," she said, "and not the things they can't do." 

     The Fairfield County Gymnastics team consists of a dozen girls. Eva Koehler doing an aerial cartwheel, upside down above, is 13 years old and has done gymnastics since she was 5; she does flips to entertain her friends.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BAY RIDGE;Anchor for More Than Boats
nytimes_articles.abstract:
John Hall's pale-brick apartment building overlooking the mouth of Sheepshead Bay is a conspicuously modern feat, wedged along the shore in a niche of modest, wood-framed houses that are home to the area's four yacht clubs. 

     "These moorings are controlled by the yacht clubs," said Mr. Hall, the president of the Sheepshead Bay Plumb Beach Civic Association. "I want something that will protect the public interest and keep the yacht clubs in business too."

Asia Without Reservations
nytimes_articles.abstract:
LAST February, our family departed for a monthlong trip to Taiwan, Bali, Singapore and Thailand with only one hotel reservation. That was in Singapore and arranged by our travel agent. 

     We were feeling footloose, and since my husband and I were traveling with our two children, both in their 20's, I felt that we should travel like 20-somethings. And most of the time, we did. We had agreed on which countries we would visit and for how long, but from that point on it we would be winging it.  In Taiwan, where our daughter, Melissa, lived, we would be in her hands, staying at her apartment or places she knew of, but a few days later we would head off to points, and accommodations, unknown.

CLASSICAL MUSIC;Levine Can't Let Go of Mozart. Or the Met.
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ON A RECENT WEEKEND at the Metropolitan Opera, a string of "Cosi Fan Tutte" performances ended, and the Mozart cast prepared to head in all directions: Carol Vaness to Houston to sing Norma, Dwayne Croft to Paris to sing Eugene Onegin, Cecilia Bartoli to Singapore to give recitals. 

     James Levine, the Met's artistic director, was to plunge immediately into final rehearsals of "Andrea Chenier" with Luciano Pavarotti, and prepare a televised gala to take place on Saturday evening commemorating the 25th anniversary of Mr. Levine's Met debut. So many of the world's greatest singers are appearing in the event that competing theaters might just as well go dark.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BAYSIDE;From the Lawn Police Files
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The lawn police watched as gardener Robert Ciampa tended to the lawn at the home of a regular client last Monday. 

     After three hours work at the house in Wexford Terrace, he piled most of the  refuse into his truck, but left behind four bags of grass clippings that he expected would be taken away by the Department of Sanitation. Instead, he was caught by inspectors from the department and later fined $200.

nytimes_articles.headline: Asia-Pacific Issue;An Island Of Serenity Near Bali
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WE were heading to Tetebatu, a hillside village in central Lombok, and already, just a few minutes from the airport, the scenery was seductive: resplendent rice fields climbing up hillsides; straw-hatted farmers wrapped to the waist in sarongs; painted donkey carts riding alongside our car. When children began appearing by the roadside to greet us with a chorus of hellos, my sister, who had traveled with me to the neighboring (and more exalted) island of Bali eight years ago, was charmed. 

     "It's so unspoiled here," she said with a sigh, "like Bali used to be."

nytimes_articles.headline: Worlds Apart
nytimes_articles.abstract: THE TEMPLE BOMBING By Melissa Fay Greene. Illustrated. 502 pp. Reading, Mass.: Addison-Wesley Publishing Company. $25.    ATLANTA: burned to ashes and skeletons during the Civil War, the town where Coca-Cola was invented in 1886, where Joel Chandler Harris wrote his Uncle Remus stories, where the usherettes wore hoop skirts at the premiere of "Gone With the Wind" in 1939. Martin Luther King Jr. was born and buried there; Atlanta was called the headquarters of the civil rights movement in the late 1950's, when he founded the Southern Christian Leadership Conference. Black students who organized the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee desegregated the lunch counters in the early 60's. Atlanta was famous for good manners even when Lester Maddox brandished an ax handle at blacks who tried to enter his fried chicken restaurant (the publicity enabled him to become Governor of Georgia in 1966).

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: FLUSHING;Science Hall's 30-Year Project
nytimes_articles.abstract:
A greatly enlarged New York Hall of Science will open its doors this week, three decades after plans for the expansion were first made. 

     The museum, the city's only hands-on science and technology center, has undergone a $13 million renovation that adds 28,400 square feet and doubles the  public space. The project adds a 300-seat auditorium, 47-foot-high glass rotunda entrance, galleries for new exhibits, an expanded gift shop and a large  dining pavilion with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking Flushing Meadows-Corona Park. A public celebration will be held at the museum Friday through next Sunday.

Mitzvah or Mania?;Party-Hopping at the High End of the Theme Bas and Bar Mitzvah
nytimes_articles.abstract:
MARCH 9 is still a hair early for an Oscar party, but on this particular Saturday night it certainly looks like that's what Laura Belle's been done up for. Two giant Oscar cut-outs survey the dance floor like flat, sexless totems,  and tropical flowers and trees on the bandstand, the tables, and the bar of this retro-glam supper club on West 43d street, welcome guests in from the cruel cold outside. There's even a replica of those arid Western hills and the famous outsized letters that rise before them, except something's not quite right here: instead of Hollywood, the sign reads, "Mollywood". 

     Indeed this is not a party to honor Oscar, but a celebration of 13-year-old Molly Lindenbaum's bas mitzvah. On this special night, the joyous end of her daylong initiation into Jewish womanhood, Molly's parents, Herb and Bonnie, have done all they can to make this event unforgettably Molly.

ASIA-PACIFIC ISSUE;A WORLD OF CHOICES: Ho CHi Mihn City
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Everybody seems to be in a hurry in Ho Chi Minh City. The streets buzz day and night as the Saigonese turn their city into an energetic, fun and slightly dangerous metropolis again. The main boulevards are dwarfed by constrcution cranes and the concrete shells of new high-rises. Sleek black BMW's and Mercedes-Benzes chauffeur Saigon's many business tycoons. Hustlers offering everything from massages to pirated CD's work the streets. 

      Traffic has accelerated. Cyclos, the bicycle-powered rickshaws, still prowl  the streets, but they are being left in the exhaust of zippy motor scooters and  new fleets of several taxicab companies, like Saigon Taxi, with its purple 1994  Mazda sedans.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: BAY RIDGE;Movie Complex Is Not a Hit
nytimes_articles.abstract:
With Brooklyn's relative dearth of movie houses, plans for a new eight-screen multiplex would have seemed a welcome announcement. At least, that's what United Artists thought when it broke ground two weeks ago on a new theater complex. 

     But the company failed to foresee how wedded Bay Ridge residents are to the quiet character of their community. So when United Artists began promoting its move into the neighborhood -- a move taken without community input -- residents  reacted as if an unwelcome visitor had come through their back door without knocking.

Books in Brief: FICTION;Furtive Glances
nytimes_articles.abstract: Mori Ogai's novel THE WILD GOOSE (Center for Japanese Studies, University of  Michigan, cloth, $28.95; paper, $14.95) is a small book that gives immense pleasure. Eloquently understated, it provides a lyrical account of the thwarted  romance between a Tokyo medical student and a young woman who is the mistress of a dull middle-aged moneylender. Written early in this century and translated here with an insightful introduction by Burton Watson, the narrative illuminates the rigid social order of Japan in the 1880's, an era when that country's rapid Westernization was just beginning. Okada, the story's protagonist, is noted for his good looks and virtue. Yet despite his heroic nature, he can't rescue Otama from her fate. The relationship of Okada and Otama consists of furtive glances, a smile, the tip of a hat -- and Okada's rescue of  a pet bird from a snake. The novel's disarming power grows from its minimal plot, its muted descriptions of the two main characters and its narrative viewpoint (the action is seen in flashback through the eyes of one of Okada's schoolmates, a technique that provides esthetic distance). Spare, symbolic imagery reinforces our understanding of all the characters, particularly the women, whose only available responses to disappointment are silent suffering and dissembling. Mr. Watson's introduction outlines the autobiographical elements in the story, and a careful reader may discern Ogai's intimations that he is writing a new kind of fiction. Certainly he was influenced by his substantial reading of European writers, and he ranks as one of the key innovators of modern Japanese literature -- but the success of "The Wild Goose" rests on its timeless portrayal of the clash between social caste and romantic love.   Gardner McFall

Legends. Champions?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
HE COACH WAS A WATCHFUL MAN, AND HE'D sensed the trouble coming. Even as his  Chicago Bulls skimmed through the N.B.A.'s marathon grind at a record-winning pace, even as they loomed as a team for the ages, Phil Jackson could feel the ions in the air long before the storm touched down in East Rutherford, N.J., on  March 19. 

     Two weeks earlier, Dennis Rodman -- the Bulls' tattooed rent-a-rebounder and  slapstick provocateur -- had whipped a Milwaukee point guard to the floor. Three games later, he did the same to a photographer's camera. There had been a rash of no-hope three-point shots, of petit mal fist-pumping and mugging to the mezzanine.

Salmon With Lemon-Herb Marinade
nytimes_articles.abstract: Here's one of the easiest ways to entertain: broil or grill a large salmon fillet, then adorn it with lemon wedges and rosemary sprigs. This recipe marinates the rich salmon in lemon juice, soy sauce and herbs. Serve it with a sharply flavored potato salad (like this one, with red Bliss potatoes and watercress in a yogurt-horseradish dressing) and a large bowl of greens.

wiki_events.headline: Four people are killed and 75 are injured in a train accident in Jokela, Finland.

pages.title: Jokela rail accident
pages.description: Train accident in Tuusula, Finland, on 21 April 1996
pages.abstract: The Jokela rail accident occurred on 21 April 1996, at 07:08 local time in Tuusula, Finland, approximately 50 kilometres (30 mi) north of Helsinki. Four people were killed and 75 injured when express train P82 from Oulu, bound for Helsinki, derailed in heavy fog. The overnight sleeper train was carrying 139 passengers and five crew members. The official investigation found the accident was caused by overspeeding through a slow-speed turnout.

pages.title: Jokela
pages.abstract: Jokela is one of the three administrative centers in the Finnish municipality Tuusula. It has a population of around 6,000 residents. The Jokela high school and the Jokela railway station serve the community. Many residents commute to the capital of Finland, Helsinki, which is about 50 kilometres (30 mi) away. The Jokela Prison, opened in 1993, is also located in the village.
